---
title: "Linear Discriminant Analysis + Decision Trees"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: serif
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    chalkboard: false # Great for drawing during stats lectures
    incremental: false # Bullet points appear one by one
    embed-resources: true
    html-math-method: mathjax 
    highlight-style: github
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

# Linear Discriminant Analysis

**Classification Technique**

## What is it?

* Multivariate [continuous] predictors [related to PCA] to many categories.

* 2 categories == Linear Discriminant Analysis OR

* 3+ categories == Canonical Variates Analysis

* Generalized Term == Discriminant Function Analysis

* Closely related to **linear regression** whereby one finds a *linear* coombination of features to separate classes.

## Goals

1.  ***Maximize*** between group differences.
2.  ***Describe*** group separation.
3.  ***Classify*** unknown cases into a known sample.

## What is it?

![](lda1.png){fig-align="center"}

## What is it?

![](lda2.png){fig-align="center"}

## What is it?

![](lda3.png){fig-align="center"}
![](lda4.png){fig-align="center"}

## What is it?

* Each **linear seperator** is simply just a function of a line. 

$$
Z = b + m1(x1) + m2(x2)
$$

* This is a **discriminant function**.
  * Function number =  1 - # of groups

* We use the value of $z$ to compare to the cutoff point - AKA decide which group one is in.

* Comparison is made with a multivariate distance - **Mahalanobis distance** $D^2$

##

$$
GOL(187)*0.11 + XCB(142)*0.107 – 17.019 = 0.286
$$

##

![](lda5.png){fig-align="center"}

## Mahalanobis Distance

* Multivariate distance metric
  * A euclidean distance in 3D using the covariance matrix.

* Adjusts for correlated data. 
  * Similar to PCA EXCEPT we maximize between group variance NOT total data variance. 
  
![](mahal1.png){fig-align="center"}

##

![](mahal2.png){fig-align="center"}

## Assumptions {.smaller}

***One output variable (group/factor) and many continuous input variables***

1.  Each case is from a mutually exclusive group
2.  Input variables are not collinear
3.  Homogeneity of variance between output groups
4.  Multivariate normally distributed samples
5.  Sufficient sample size: The smallest sample size should be 3x the number of input variables
    * Ex: 20 input/predictor variables is n≥60, per group 

## How an LDA works

* The Mahalanobis D2 examines the squared distance from an individual case to the group centroid
* An individual will be classified into the group with the lowest D2
* An individual will be forced to classify into a group, even if the actual group is not in the reference data or if it is far from all centroids
    * Reference data is important!
* Data are always scaled before being used!

## DFA vs. Logistic

* Can discriminate between >2 groups
* Performs much better when groups are well-separated
* More stable for small sample sizes

## DFA in R

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| message: false

library(MASS) #LDA
library(tidyverse)
library(magrittr)

data("iris")
iris2 <- iris
names(iris2) <- c("sl","sw","pl","pw","species")
iris2 %<>% mutate_at(names(iris2)[-5], scale)  # don’t forget to scale!

```

##

```{r}
#| echo: true

iris_lda <- lda(species ~., data=iris2)  # what does ~. mean?
iris_lda

```

## Remember, DFA is Predictive!

```{r}
#| echo: true

# pred <- predict(iris_lda, newdata = ...)
pred <- predict(iris_lda)
names(pred)
```

* ***Class***: class or group each row is classified into.

* ***Posterior***: Posterior probablity of belonging to each group

* **x** is values of each discrimnant score in standardized space.

##

```{r}
#| echo: true

head(pred$class)

head(pred$posterior)

head(pred$x)
```


##

```{r}
#| echo: true

plot(iris_lda)

```

##

```{r}
#| echo: true

all_iris <- cbind(pred$x, iris2)

p <- ggplot(all_iris, aes(x=LD1, y=LD2)) + 	theme_bw() + 	geom_point(aes(color=species), 	size=3) + 	
	stat_ellipse(
	aes(linetype=species)) + 	
	labs(x="CV Score 1 (99%)", 
	y="CV Score 2 (0.8%)", 	color="Species", lty="Species")

```

##

```{r}
#| echo: true

p
```

## Add Centroids

```{r}
#| echo: true

# Calculate centroids:
ld1 <- pred$x[,1] 
ld2 <- pred$x[,2]

cent <- data.frame(matrix(nrow=3, ncol=3))
names(cent) <- c("species","LD1","LD2")
specs <- as.character(unique(iris2$species))

for(s in 1:3) {  
	cent[s, "species"] <- specs[s]  
	cent[s, "LD1"] <-sum(ld1*(iris2$species==specs[s]))/sum(iris2$species==specs[s])  	
	cent[s, "LD2"] <- sum(ld2*(iris2$species==specs[s]))/sum(iris2$species==specs[s])
}

cent

```

##

```{r}
#| echo: true

# Add Centroids to Plot:
p + geom_point(data=cent, aes(x=LD1, y=LD2, 	shape=species), size=6) + 
	guides(shape="none")

```

## Classification Tables

```{r}
#| echo: true

# Create a contingency table with known(rows) and predicted (cols)
ct <- table(pred$class, iris2$species)
ct

# Calculate the percent correct for each species:
diag(prop.table(ct, 1))

# Sum the diagonal proportions for overall correct classification:
sum(diag(prop.table(ct)))

```

## Confusion Matrices!

```{r}
library(caret)
confusionMatrix(ct)
```
